findings.location: BSR_Compatibility::get_sysinfo(), line 51; rendered in templates/bsr-help.php line 62
findings.reachability: Admin-only: the help tab is only accessible to users with admin/manage_options capability. SERVER_SOFTWARE is set by the web server, not directly by end-users, but could be influenced by a malicious reverse proxy or misconfigured server.
findings.justification: Line 51 concatenates $_SERVER['SERVER_SOFTWARE'] into $return without any escaping; the entire $return string is then echoed raw into an HTML <textarea> at bsr-help.php:62 (echo BSR_Compatibility::get_sysinfo()) without esc_textarea() or esc_html(), so a value like '</textarea><script>alert(1)</script>' in SERVER_SOFTWARE would break out of the textarea and execute JS in the admin context.

detection_pattern.section_name: XSS: unescaped $_SERVER superglobal echoed in HTML
detection_pattern.description: Catches direct echo of $_SERVER values without output escaping; in WordPress admin pages these can break out of HTML contexts (including textarea) if server-controlled headers like SERVER_SOFTWARE contain HTML metacharacters.

findings.reachability: Admin-only: only users who can access the BSR help/system-info admin tab.
findings.justification: The call 'echo BSR_Compatibility::get_sysinfo()' outputs an aggregate string built from multiple server/environment sources directly into a <textarea> without esc_textarea(); WordPress coding standards require esc_textarea() for any value placed between <textarea> tags to prevent HTML injection even for trusted data, because any source in the aggregate that contains '</textarea>' would break the element boundary.

detection_pattern.section_name: XSS: textarea content echoed without esc_textarea()
detection_pattern.description: Matches PHP echo inside <textarea> tags without esc_textarea() wrapping; any unescaped value that contains '</textarea>' can break out of the element and inject arbitrary HTML/JS, even in admin-only contexts.

findings.reachability: Authenticated admin only; CSRF is prevented by nonce check on line 279 (check_admin_referer). An admin who crafts a raw POST request can supply arbitrary content in $_POST['bsr-sysinfo'].
findings.justification: Line 288 echoes wp_strip_all_tags($_POST['bsr-sysinfo']) as the body of a text/plain download; the server-generated sysinfo is placed in a <textarea> in the form but the POST handler trusts that value from the client, so an admin (or a script with admin credentials) can substitute any text content in the downloaded 'system info' file, making the file unreliable as evidence for support or forensics.

detection_pattern.section_name: Information integrity: admin download reflecting user-supplied POST data
detection_pattern.description: Catches echo of wp_strip_all_tags($_POST[...]) in download handlers; while not directly exploitable for code execution, it means the downloaded 'server info' file content is attacker-controlled by any authenticated admin, undermining its value as a trusted diagnostic artifact.
